title: Presbyterian

infobox.key: [Reformation Wall](/wiki/Reformation_Wall "Reformation Wall") in Geneva, featuring prominent Reformed theologians [William Farel](/wiki/William_Farel "William Farel"), [John Calvin](/wiki/John_Calvin "John Calvin"), [Theodore Beza](/wiki/Theodore_Beza "Theodore Beza"), and [John Knox](/wiki/John_Knox "John Knox")

infobox.value: ([*pres-bə-TE-ree-a-nizm*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")) is a historically [Reformed](/wiki/Reformed_Christianity "Reformed Christianity") [Protestant](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") tradition named after its form of [church government](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_polity "Ecclesiastical polity") by representative assemblies of [elders](/wiki/Presbyterian_polity#Elder "Presbyterian polity"), known as "presbyters".[[2]](#cite_note-2) Though other Reformed churches are structurally similar, the word *Presbyterian* is applied to churches that trace their roots to the [Church of Scotland](/wiki/Church_of_Scotland "Church of Scotland") or to [English Dissenter groups](/wiki/English_Dissenters "English Dissenters") that were formed during the [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War"), 1642 to 1651.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

**Presbyterianism** ([*pres-bə-TE-ree-a-nizm*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")) is a historically [Reformed](/wiki/Reformed_Christianity "Reformed Christianity") [Protestant](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") tradition named after its form of [church government](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_polity "Ecclesiastical polity") by representative assemblies of [elders](/wiki/Presbyterian_polity#Elder "Presbyterian polity"), known as "presbyters".[[2]](#cite_note-2) Though other Reformed churches are structurally similar, the word *Presbyterian* is applied to churches that trace their roots to the [Church of Scotland](/wiki/Church_of_Scotland "Church of Scotland") or to [English Dissenter groups](/wiki/English_Dissenters "English Dissenters") that were formed during the [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War"), 1642 to 1651.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Presbyterian theology typically emphasises the [sovereignty of God](/wiki/Sovereignty_of_God "Sovereignty of God"), the [authority of the Scriptures](/wiki/Sola_scriptura "Sola scriptura"), and the necessity of [grace](/wiki/Grace_in_Christianity "Grace in Christianity") through [faith](/wiki/Faith_in_Christianity "Faith in Christianity") in Christ. Scotland ensured Presbyterian church government in the 1707 [Acts of Union](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1707 "Acts of Union 1707"),[[4]](#cite_note-4) which created the [Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain"). In fact, most Presbyterians in England have a Scottish connection. The Presbyterian denomination was also taken to [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand") and [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa"), mostly by [Scots](/wiki/Scottish_people "Scottish people") and [Scots-Irish](/wiki/Ulster_Scots_people "Ulster Scots people") immigrants.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Scotland's Presbyterian denominations hold to the [Reformed theology](/wiki/Reformed_theology "Reformed theology") of [John Calvin](/wiki/John_Calvin "John Calvin") and his immediate successors, although there is a range of theological views within contemporary Presbyterianism. Local congregations of churches that use [Presbyterian polity](/wiki/Presbyterian_polity "Presbyterian polity") are governed by [sessions](/wiki/Session_(Presbyterianism) "Session (Presbyterianism)") made up of representatives of the congregation ([elders](/wiki/Presbyterian_elder "Presbyterian elder")), a [conciliar](/wiki/Conciliarity "Conciliarity") approach as with other levels of decision-making ([presbytery](/wiki/Presbytery_(church_polity) "Presbytery (church polity)"), [synod](/wiki/Presbyterian_synod "Presbyterian synod"), and [general assembly](/wiki/General_Assembly_(presbyterian_church) "General Assembly (presbyterian church)")). There are roughly 75 million Presbyterians in the world.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Presbyterianism's roots lie in the [Magisterial Reformation](/wiki/Magisterial_Reformation "Magisterial Reformation") of the 16th century. John Calvin's [Republic of Geneva](/wiki/Republic_of_Geneva "Republic of Geneva") was particularly influential, along with Calvin's student, [Scottish Reformer](/wiki/Scottish_Reformation "Scottish Reformation") [John Knox](/wiki/John_Knox "John Knox") who worked with civil magistrates to establish the Presbyterian Church in Scotland, writing the book of [common order](/wiki/Book_of_Common_Order "Book of Common Order") and eventually the [Scots Confession](/wiki/Scots_Confession "Scots Confession").[[7]](#cite_note-7) Most Reformed churches that trace their history to Scotland are either presbyterian or [congregationalist](/wiki/Congregational_polity "Congregational polity") in government. In the 20th century, some Presbyterians played an important role in the [ecumenical movement](/wiki/Ecumenism "Ecumenism"), including the [World Council of Churches](/wiki/World_Council_of_Churches "World Council of Churches"). Many Presbyterian denominations have found ways of working together with other Reformed denominations and Christians of other traditions, especially in the [World Communion of Reformed Churches](/wiki/World_Communion_of_Reformed_Churches "World Communion of Reformed Churches"). Some Presbyterian churches have entered into [unions](/wiki/United_and_uniting_churches "United and uniting churches") with other churches, such as the [Continental Reformed](/wiki/Continental_Reformed_Protestantism "Continental Reformed Protestantism"), [Congregationalists](/wiki/Congregational_church "Congregational church"), [Lutherans](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism"), [Anglicans](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism"), and [Methodists](/wiki/Methodists "Methodists"). Presbyterians in the United States came largely from [Scottish](/wiki/Scottish_American "Scottish American"), [Scots-Irish immigrants](/wiki/Scotch-Irish_American "Scotch-Irish American"), and also from [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England") communities that were originally [Congregational](/wiki/Congregationalism_in_the_United_States "Congregationalism in the United States") but changed because of an agreed-upon [Plan of Union of 1801](/wiki/Plan_of_Union_of_1801 "Plan of Union of 1801") for frontier areas.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

### Early history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presbyterianism&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early history")]

Main article: [Christianisation of Scotland](/wiki/Christianisation_of_Scotland "Christianisation of Scotland")

Presbyterian tradition, particularly that of the [Church of Scotland](/wiki/Church_of_Scotland "Church of Scotland"), traces its early roots to the Catholic church of Scotland. It was founded by [Saint Columba](/wiki/Saint_Columba "Saint Columba"), through the 6th century [Hiberno-Scottish mission](/wiki/Hiberno-Scottish_mission "Hiberno-Scottish mission").[[9]](#cite_note-Atkins2016-9)[[10]](#cite_note-TaylorAnderson1852-10)[[11]](#cite_note-Bradley2013-11) Tracing their apostolic origin to [Saint John](/wiki/John_the_Apostle "John the Apostle"),[[12]](#cite_note-DickensLewis1920-12)[[13]](#cite_note-Thomson1896-13) the [Culdees](/wiki/Culdees "Culdees") practiced [Christian monasticism](/wiki/Christian_monasticism "Christian monasticism"), a key feature of [Celtic Christianity](/wiki/Celtic_Christianity "Celtic Christianity") in the region, with a [presbyter](/wiki/Presbyter "Presbyter") exercising "authority within the institution, while the different monastic institutions were independent of one another."[[14]](#cite_note-Mackay1902-14)[[9]](#cite_note-Atkins2016-9)[[15]](#cite_note-HannrachainArmstrong2014-15) The Church in Scotland kept the Christian feast of Easter at a date different from the [See of Rome](/wiki/See_of_Rome "See of Rome") and its monks used a unique style of [tonsure](/wiki/Tonsure "Tonsure").[[16]](#cite_note-Rankin1884-16) The [Synod of Whitby](/wiki/Synod_of_Whitby "Synod of Whitby") in 664, however, ended these distinctions as it ruled "that Easter would be celebrated according to the Roman date, not the Celtic date."[[17]](#cite_note-Skinner1999-17) Although Roman influence came to dominate the Church in Scotland,[[17]](#cite_note-Skinner1999-17) certain Celtic influences remained in the Scottish Church,[[18]](#cite_note-Eggins2015-18) such as "the singing of [metrical psalms](/wiki/Metrical_psalms "Metrical psalms"), many of them set to old Celtic Christianity Scottish traditional and folk tunes", which later became a "distinctive part of Scottish Presbyterian worship".[[19]](#cite_note-Bowden2005-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)

### Development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presbyterianism&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Development")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/John_Knox_woodcut.jpg/250px-John_Knox_woodcut.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_Knox_woodcut.jpg)

[John Knox](/wiki/John_Knox "John Knox"), leader of the 16th century [Scottish Reformation](/wiki/Scottish_Reformation "Scottish Reformation")

Presbyterian history is part of the [history of Christianity](/wiki/History_of_Christianity "History of Christianity"), but the beginning of Presbyterianism as a distinct movement occurred during the 16th century [Protestant Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation"). As the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") resisted the Reformers, several different theological movements splintered from the Church and bore different denominations. The Presbyterian Church (The Church of Scotland) was a direct break off of the Roman Catholic Church as were the Anglican Church and the Lutheran Church.

Presbyterianism was especially influenced by the French theologian [John Calvin](/wiki/John_Calvin "John Calvin"), who is credited with the development of [Reformed theology](/wiki/Reformed_theology "Reformed theology"), and the work of [John Knox](/wiki/John_Knox "John Knox"), a Scottish Catholic Priest who studied with Calvin in Geneva and brought back Reformed teachings to Scotland. An important influence on the formation of Presbyterianism in Britain also came from [John a Lasco](/wiki/Jan_%C5%81aski "Jan Łaski"), a Polish reformer, the founder of a [Stranger's Church](/wiki/Stranger_churches "Stranger churches") in London, based on the Geneva models.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

The Presbyterian church traces its ancestry primarily to Scotland. In August 1560, the [Parliament of Scotland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Scotland "Parliament of Scotland") adopted the [Scots Confession](/wiki/Scots_Confession "Scots Confession") as the creed of the Scottish Kingdom. In December 1560, the *[First Book of Discipline](/wiki/First_Book_of_Discipline "First Book of Discipline")* was published, outlining important doctrinal issues but also establishing regulations for church government, including the creation of ten ecclesiastical districts with appointed superintendents which later became known as [presbyteries](/wiki/Presbytery_(church_polity) "Presbytery (church polity)").[[22]](#cite_note-22)

In time, the Scots Confession would be supplanted by the [Westminster Confession of Faith](/wiki/Westminster_Confession_of_Faith "Westminster Confession of Faith"), and the [larger](/wiki/Westminster_Larger_Catechism "Westminster Larger Catechism") and [shorter catechisms](/wiki/Westminster_Shorter_Catechism "Westminster Shorter Catechism"), which were formulated by the [Westminster Assembly](/wiki/Westminster_Assembly "Westminster Assembly") between 1643 and 1649.

## Characteristics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presbyterianism&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Characteristics")]

Presbyterians distinguish themselves from other denominations by [doctrine](/wiki/Doctrine "Doctrine"), institutional organisation (or "church order") and [worship](/wiki/Worship "Worship"), often using a "Book of Order" to regulate common practice and order. The origins of the Presbyterian churches are in [Reformed Christianity](/wiki/Reformed_Christianity "Reformed Christianity"), also called Calvinism. Many branches of Presbyterianism are remnants of previous splits from larger groups. Some of the splits have been due to doctrinal controversy, while some have been caused by disagreement concerning the degree to which those ordained to church office should be required to agree with the [Westminster Confession of Faith](/wiki/Westminster_Confession_of_Faith "Westminster Confession of Faith"), which historically serves as an important confessional document—second only to the Bible, yet directing particularities in the standardisation and translation of the Bible—in Presbyterian churches.

Presbyterians place importance on education and lifelong learning, tempered with the belief that no human action can affect [salvation](/wiki/Salvation_in_Christianity "Salvation in Christianity").

Continuous study of the scriptures, theological writings, and understanding and interpretation of church doctrine are embodied in several statements of faith and [catechisms](/wiki/Catechism "Catechism") formally adopted by various branches of the church, often referred to as "[subordinate standards](/wiki/Subordinate_standard "Subordinate standard")".

### Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presbyterianism&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Government")]

Main article: [Presbyterian church governance](/wiki/Presbyterian_church_governance "Presbyterian church governance")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Lorimer%2C_Ordination.jpg/250px-Lorimer%2C_Ordination.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lorimer,_Ordination.jpg)

Painting by [John Henry Lorimer](/wiki/John_Henry_Lorimer "John Henry Lorimer"), *The Ordination of Elders in a Scottish Kirk*, 1891, now in the [National Gallery of Scotland](/wiki/National_Gallery_of_Scotland "National Gallery of Scotland")

Presbyterian government is by councils (still known as *courts* in some countries, as *boards* in others) of elders. Teaching and ruling elders are ordained and convene in the lowest council known as a [*session*](/wiki/Session_(Presbyterianism) "Session (Presbyterianism)") or *[consistory](/wiki/Consistory_(Protestantism)#Reformed_usage "Consistory (Protestantism)")* responsible for the discipline, nurture, and mission of the local [congregation](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/congregation "wiktionary:congregation"). Teaching elders (pastors or ministers) have responsibility for teaching, worship, and performing sacraments. Pastors or ministers are called by individual congregations. A congregation issues a call for the pastor or minister's service, but this call must be ratified by the local presbytery. The pastor or minister is a teaching elder, and Moderator of the Session, but is not usually a member of the congregation; instead, this person is a member of the Presbytery of which the given church is a member.

Ruling elders are elected by the congregation and ordained to serve with the teaching elders, assuming responsibility for the nurture and leadership of the congregation. Often, especially in larger congregations, the elders delegate the practicalities of buildings, finance, and temporal ministry to the needy in the congregation to a distinct group of officers (sometimes called deacons, which are ordained in some denominations). This group may variously be known as a "Deacon Board", "Board of Deacons" "Diaconate", or "Deacons' Court". These are sometimes known as "presbyters" to the full congregation. Since the 20th century, most denominations allow women to be teaching or ruling elders.

Above the sessions exist presbyteries, which have area responsibilities. These are composed of teaching elders and ruling elders from each of the constituent congregations. The presbytery sends representatives to a broader regional or national assembly, generally known as the [General Assembly](/wiki/General_Assembly_(presbyterian_church) "General Assembly (presbyterian church)"), although an intermediate level of a *[synod](/wiki/Synod "Synod")* sometimes exists. This congregation / [presbytery](/wiki/Presbytery_(presbyterian_church) "Presbytery (presbyterian church)") / [synod](/wiki/Synod "Synod") / [general assembly](/wiki/General_Assembly_(presbyterian_church) "General Assembly (presbyterian church)") schema is based on the historical structure of the larger Presbyterian churches, such as the [Church of Scotland](/wiki/Church_of_Scotland "Church of Scotland") or the [Presbyterian Church (U.S.A.)](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_(U.S.A.) "Presbyterian Church (U.S.A.)"); some bodies, such as the [Presbyterian Church in America](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_in_America "Presbyterian Church in America") and the [Presbyterian Church in Ireland](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_in_Ireland "Presbyterian Church in Ireland"), skip one of the steps between congregation and General Assembly, and usually the step skipped is the Synod. The [Church of Scotland](/wiki/Church_of_Scotland "Church of Scotland") abolished the Synod in 1993.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

Presbyterian governance is practiced by Presbyterian denominations and also by many other [Reformed churches](/wiki/Reformed_churches "Reformed churches").[[24]](#cite_note-24)

### Doctrine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presbyterianism&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Doctrine")]

See also: [Reformed theology](/wiki/Reformed_theology "Reformed theology")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/Celtic_cross_draped_for_Easter.jpg/250px-Celtic_cross_draped_for_Easter.jpg)](/wiki/File:Celtic_cross_draped_for_Easter.jpg)

A Celtic cross draped for [Easter](/wiki/Easter "Easter") at a Presbyterian church in [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia "Virginia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/USVA_headstone_emb-04.svg/250px-USVA_headstone_emb-04.svg.png)](/wiki/File:USVA_headstone_emb-04.svg)

A Presbyterian Cross, used by the [National Cemetery Administration](/w/index.php?title=United_States_National_Cemetery_Administration&action=edit&redlink=1 "United States National Cemetery Administration (page does not exist)"), a division of the [U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Veterans_Affairs "United States Department of Veterans Affairs")[[25]](#cite_note-25)

Presbyterianism is historically a confessional tradition. Confessional churches express their faith in the form of "confessions of faith", which have some level of authoritative status. In confessional churches, theology is not solely an individual matter. While individuals are encouraged to understand Scripture, and may challenge the current institutional understanding, theology is carried out by the community as a whole. It is this community understanding of theology that is expressed in confessions.[[26]](#cite_note-lostsoul-26)

There is a spectrum of approaches to [confessionalism](/wiki/Confessionalism_(religion) "Confessionalism (religion)"). The manner of [subscription](/wiki/Confessional_subscription "Confessional subscription"), or the degree to which the official standards establish the actual doctrine of the church, is a practical matter leading to the decisions rendered in ordination and in the courts of the church to largely determine what the church means, representing the whole, by its adherence to the doctrinal standard.

Some Presbyterian traditions adopt only the [Westminster Confession of Faith](/wiki/Westminster_Confession_of_Faith "Westminster Confession of Faith") as the doctrinal standard to which teaching elders are required to subscribe, in contrast to the [Larger](/wiki/Westminster_Larger_Catechism "Westminster Larger Catechism") and [Shorter](/wiki/Westminster_Shorter_Catechism "Westminster Shorter Catechism") catechisms, which are approved for use in instruction. Many Presbyterian denominations, especially in North America, have adopted all of the [Westminster Standards](/wiki/Westminster_Standards "Westminster Standards") as their standard of doctrine which is subordinate to the Bible. These documents are [Calvinist](/wiki/Calvinist "Calvinist") in their doctrinal orientation. The [Presbyterian Church in Canada](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_in_Canada "Presbyterian Church in Canada") retains the Westminster Confession of Faith in its original form, while admitting the historical period in which it was written should be understood when it is read.

The Westminster Confession is "The principal [subordinate standard](/wiki/Subordinate_standard "Subordinate standard") of the [Church of Scotland](/wiki/Church_of_Scotland "Church of Scotland")" but "with due regard to liberty of opinion in points which do not enter into the substance of the Faith" (V).[[27]](#cite_note-27) This formulation represents many years of struggle over the extent to which the confession reflects the Word of God and the struggle of conscience of those who came to believe it did not fully do so (e.g. [William Robertson Smith](/wiki/William_Robertson_Smith "William Robertson Smith")). Some Presbyterian churches, such as the [Free Church of Scotland](/wiki/Free_Church_of_Scotland_(post_1900) "Free Church of Scotland (post 1900)"), have no such "[conscience](/wiki/Conscience "Conscience") clause".

The [Presbyterian Church (U.S.A.)](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_(U.S.A.) "Presbyterian Church (U.S.A.)") has adopted the *[Book of Confessions](/wiki/Book_of_Confessions "Book of Confessions")*, which reflects the inclusion of other [Reformed confessions](/wiki/Reformed_confessions "Reformed confessions") in addition to the Westminster Standards. These other documents include ancient creedal statements (the [Nicene Creed](/wiki/Nicene_Creed "Nicene Creed"), the [Apostles' Creed](/wiki/Apostles%27_Creed "Apostles' Creed")), 16th-century Reformed confessions (the [Scots Confession](/wiki/Scots_Confession "Scots Confession"), the [Heidelberg Catechism](/wiki/Heidelberg_Catechism "Heidelberg Catechism"), the [Second Helvetic Confession](/wiki/Second_Helvetic_Confession "Second Helvetic Confession")), and 20th century documents ([The Theological Declaration of Barmen](/wiki/Barmen_Declaration "Barmen Declaration"), [Confession of 1967](/wiki/Confession_of_1967 "Confession of 1967") and [A Brief Statement of Faith](/wiki/A_Brief_Statement_of_Faith "A Brief Statement of Faith")).

The Presbyterian Church in Canada developed the confessional document *Living Faith* (1984) and retains it as a subordinate standard of the denomination. It is confessional in format, yet like the Westminster Confession, draws attention to original Bible text.

Presbyterians in Ireland who rejected Calvinism and the Westminster Confessions formed the [Non-subscribing Presbyterian Church of Ireland](/wiki/Non-subscribing_Presbyterian_Church_of_Ireland "Non-subscribing Presbyterian Church of Ireland").

[John Gresham Machen](/wiki/J._Gresham_Machen "J. Gresham Machen"), the prominent Presbyterian theologian and Professor of [New Testament](/wiki/New_Testament "New Testament") at [Princeton Seminary](/wiki/Princeton_Seminary "Princeton Seminary") between 1906 and 1929, led a revolt against modernist doctrine in his *Christianity and Liberalism* (1923) that critiqued theological modernism. He argued that modernism and liberal theology was a false religion, a pretender that cloaks itself in Christian language – "Liberalism". This religion, he claimed, is a marriage of naturalism, humanism, secularism, and sentimentalism all rolled into one.

### Worship and sacraments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presbyterianism&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Worship and sacraments")]

#### Worship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presbyterianism&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Worship")]

Main article: [Presbyterian worship](/wiki/Presbyterian_worship "Presbyterian worship")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/Presbyterian_Catechising.jpg/250px-Presbyterian_Catechising.jpg)](/wiki/File:Presbyterian_Catechising.jpg)

Presbyterian [catechising](/wiki/Catechesis "Catechesis") in the 19th century

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/A_Scottish_Sacrament.jpg/250px-A_Scottish_Sacrament.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_Scottish_Sacrament.jpg)

*A Scottish Sacrament*, a portrait by Henry John Dobson

Presbyterian denominations that trace their heritage to the British Isles usually organise their church services inspired by the principles in the [Directory of Public Worship](/wiki/Directory_of_Public_Worship "Directory of Public Worship"), developed by the [Westminster Assembly](/wiki/Westminster_Assembly "Westminster Assembly") in the 1640s. This directory documented [Reformed worship](/wiki/Reformed_worship "Reformed worship") practices and theology adopted and developed over the preceding century by British [Puritans](/wiki/Puritan "Puritan"), initially guided by John Calvin and John Knox. It was enacted as law by the [Parliament of Scotland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Scotland "Parliament of Scotland"), and became one of the foundational documents of Presbyterian church legislation elsewhere.

Historically, the driving principle in the development of the standards of Presbyterian worship is the [Regulative principle of worship](/wiki/Regulative_principle_of_worship "Regulative principle of worship"), which specifies that (in worship), what is not commanded is forbidden.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

Over subsequent centuries, many Presbyterian churches modified these prescriptions by introducing hymnody, instrumental accompaniment, and ceremonial [vestments](/wiki/Vestment "Vestment") into worship. However, there is not one fixed "Presbyterian" worship style. Although there are set services for the [Lord's Day](/wiki/Lord%27s_Day "Lord's Day") in keeping with [first-day Sabbatarianism](/wiki/First-day_Sabbatarianism "First-day Sabbatarianism"),[[29]](#cite_note-Wigley1980-29) one can find a service to be evangelical and even revivalist in tone (especially in some conservative denominations), or strongly liturgical, approximating the practices of [Lutheranism](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") or more of [Anglicanism](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism"),[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] or semi-formal, allowing for a balance of hymns, preaching, and congregational participation (favored by many American Presbyterians). Most Presbyterian churches follow the traditional liturgical year and observe the traditional holidays, holy seasons, such as [Advent](/wiki/Advent "Advent"), Christmas, [Ash Wednesday](/wiki/Ash_Wednesday "Ash Wednesday"), [Holy Week](/wiki/Holy_Week "Holy Week"), Easter, [Pentecost](/wiki/Pentecost "Pentecost"), etc. They also make use of the appropriate [seasonal liturgical colors](/wiki/Liturgical_colours#Reformed_churches "Liturgical colours"), etc. Many incorporate ancient liturgical prayers and responses into the communion services and follow a daily, seasonal, and festival lectionary. Other Presbyterians, however, such as the [Reformed Presbyterians](/wiki/Reformed_Presbyterian_churches "Reformed Presbyterian churches"), would practice [a cappella](/wiki/A_cappella "A cappella") [exclusive psalmody](/wiki/Exclusive_psalmody "Exclusive psalmody"), as well as eschew the celebration of holy days.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/First_Presbyterian_Church_of_Pittsburgh.png/250px-First_Presbyterian_Church_of_Pittsburgh.png)](/wiki/File:First_Presbyterian_Church_of_Pittsburgh.png)

[First Presbyterian Church of Pittsburgh](/wiki/First_Presbyterian_Church_(Pittsburgh) "First Presbyterian Church (Pittsburgh)") (Conservative PCUSA Church)

Among the [paleo-orthodox](/wiki/Paleo-orthodoxy "Paleo-orthodoxy") and [emerging church](/wiki/Emerging_church "Emerging church") movements in Protestant and evangelical churches, in which some Presbyterians are involved, clergy are moving away from the traditional black [Geneva gown](/wiki/Geneva_gown "Geneva gown") to such vestments as the [alb](/wiki/Alb "Alb") and [chasuble](/wiki/Chasuble "Chasuble"), but also [cassock](/wiki/Cassock "Cassock") and [surplice](/wiki/Surplice "Surplice") (typically a full-length Old English style surplice which resembles the [Celtic](/wiki/Celts "Celts") alb, an ungirdled liturgical tunic of the old [Gallican Rite](/wiki/Gallican_Rite "Gallican Rite")), which some, particularly those identifying with the Liturgical Renewal Movement, hold to be more ancient and representative of a more ecumenical past.

#### Sacraments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presbyterianism&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Sacraments")]

See also: [Reformed baptismal theology](/wiki/Reformed_baptismal_theology "Reformed baptismal theology") and [Lord's Supper in Reformed theology](/wiki/Lord%27s_Supper_in_Reformed_theology "Lord's Supper in Reformed theology")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Presbyterian_Church_Communion_Table.jpg/250px-Presbyterian_Church_Communion_Table.jpg)](/wiki/File:Presbyterian_Church_Communion_Table.jpg)

Presbyterian Church Communion Table

Presbyterians traditionally have held the Worship position that there are only two [sacraments](/wiki/Sacraments "Sacraments"):

* [Baptism](/wiki/Baptism "Baptism"), in which they [baptize infants](/wiki/Infant_baptism "Infant baptism"), as well as unbaptized adults by the [Aspersion](/wiki/Aspersion "Aspersion") (sprinkling) or [Affusion](/wiki/Affusion "Affusion") (pouring) method in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit, rather than the [Immersion](/wiki/Baptism#Immersion "Baptism") method.
* The [Lord's Supper](/wiki/Eucharist "Eucharist") (also known as Communion), in which Presbyterians believe in the Real Presence of Christ (pneumatic presence) in the spiritual sense, in the bread and wine through the Holy Spirit, as opposed to being locally present as in [transubstantiation](/wiki/Transubstantiation "Transubstantiation").

[Coral Ridge Presbyterian Church](/wiki/Coral_Ridge_Presbyterian_Church "Coral Ridge Presbyterian Church"), built in the 1970s, an example of modern church architecture

Some early Presbyterians, which were influenced by the puritan movement, were careful to distinguish between the "church", which referred to the *members*, and the "meeting house", which was the building in which the church met. (Quakers still insist upon this distinction.) Until the late 19th century, very few Presbyterians ever referred to their buildings as "churches". Presbyterians believed that meeting-houses (now called churches) are buildings to support the worship of God. The [decor](/wiki/Interior_decoration "Interior decoration") in some instances was austere so as not to detract from worship. Early Presbyterian meeting-houses were extremely plain. No stained glass, no elaborate furnishings, and no images were to be found in the meeting-house. The pulpit, often raised so as only to be accessible by a staircase, was the centerpiece of the building. But these were not the standard characteristics of the mainline Presbyterians. These were more of the wave of Presbyterians that were influenced by the Puritans.

In the late 19th century a gradual shift began to occur. Prosperous congregations built imposing churches, such as [Fourth Presbyterian Church of Chicago](/wiki/Fourth_Presbyterian_Church_of_Chicago "Fourth Presbyterian Church of Chicago"), [Brick Presbyterian Church](/wiki/Brick_Presbyterian_Church_(New_York_City) "Brick Presbyterian Church (New York City)") in New York City, [Shadyside Presbyterian Church](/wiki/Shadyside_Presbyterian_Church "Shadyside Presbyterian Church") in Pennsylvania, St Stephen Presbyterian in Fort Worth, Texas, and many others.

While Presbyterian churches historically reflected prevailing architectural trends, the 20th century saw a greater embrace of modern architectural styles, particularly the [modernist movement](/wiki/Modern_architecture "Modern architecture") characterized by clean lines, geometric shapes, and open floor plans.[[30]](#cite_note-30) Prominent examples include [Coral Ridge Presbyterian Church](/wiki/Coral_Ridge_Presbyterian_Church "Coral Ridge Presbyterian Church") in Fort Lauderdale, Florida, with expansive, light-filled sanctuary and angular design elements. Similarly, the [National Presbyterian Church](/wiki/National_Presbyterian_Church "National Presbyterian Church") in Washington, D.C., features a striking facade clad in limestone and punctuated by large windows, alongside abstract stained-glass windows. Both of these were designed by famed architect [Harold E. Wagoner](/wiki/Harold_E._Wagoner "Harold E. Wagoner").

Usually a Presbyterian church will not have statues of saints, nor the ornate altar more typical of a [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") church. Instead, there is a "communion table", usually on the same level as the congregation, and sometimes elevated similar to an altar, however surrounded by the chancel. There may be a rail between the communion table and the chancel behind it, which may contain a more decorative altar-type table, choir loft, or choir stalls, lectern and clergy area. The altar is called the communion table, and the altar area is called the chancel by Presbyterians. In Presbyterian, and in Reformed churches, there may be an altar cross, either on the communion table or on a table in the chancel. By using the "empty" cross, or cross of the Westminster/Celtic cross, Presbyterians emphasize the resurrection and that Christ is not continually dying, but died once and is alive for all eternity. Quite a few Presbyterian church buildings are decorated with a cross, that has a circle around the center, or Celtic cross. This not only emphasizes the resurrection, but also acknowledges historical aspects of Presbyterianism. A baptismal font will be located either at the entrance or near the chancel area. Presbyterian architecture generally makes significant use of symbolism. One may also find decorative and ornate stained glass windows depicting scenes from the Bible. Some Presbyterian churches will also have ornate statues of Christ or graven scenes from the Last Supper located behind the chancel. [St. Giles' Cathedral](/wiki/St._Giles%27_Cathedral "St. Giles' Cathedral") in Scotland has a crucifix next to an ornate elevated communion table that hangs alongside. The image of Christ is more of a faint image, with a more modern design.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

## By region

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presbyterianism&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: By region")]

### Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presbyterianism&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Europe")]

#### Scotland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presbyterianism&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Scotland")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Covenanters_in_a_Glen.jpg/250px-Covenanters_in_a_Glen.jpg)](/wiki/File:Covenanters_in_a_Glen.jpg)

*Covenanters in a Glen*, a depiction of an illegal [conventicle](/wiki/Conventicle "Conventicle")

[John Knox](/wiki/John_Knox "John Knox") (1505–1572), a [Scot](/wiki/Scottish_people "Scottish people") who had spent time studying under [Calvin](/wiki/John_Calvin "John Calvin") in Geneva, returned to [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland") and urged his countrymen to reform the Church in line with [Calvinist](/wiki/Calvinism "Calvinism") doctrines. After a period of religious convulsion and political conflict culminating in a victory for the [Protestant party](/wiki/Lords_of_the_Congregation "Lords of the Congregation") at the [Siege of Leith](/wiki/Siege_of_Leith "Siege of Leith") the authority of the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") was abolished in favour of [Reformation](/wiki/Scottish_Reformation "Scottish Reformation") by the legislation of the [Scottish Reformation Parliament](/wiki/Scottish_Reformation_Parliament "Scottish Reformation Parliament") in 1560. The Church was eventually organised by [Andrew Melville](/wiki/Andrew_Melville "Andrew Melville") along Presbyterian lines to become the national [Church of Scotland](/wiki/Church_of_Scotland "Church of Scotland"). [King James VI and I](/wiki/King_James_VI_and_I "King James VI and I") moved the Church of Scotland towards an episcopal form of government, and in 1637, James' successor, [Charles I](/wiki/Charles_I_of_England "Charles I of England") and [William Laud](/wiki/William_Laud "William Laud"), the [Archbishop of Canterbury](/wiki/Archbishop_of_Canterbury "Archbishop of Canterbury"), attempted to force the Church of Scotland to use the [Book of Common Prayer](/wiki/Book_of_Common_Prayer "Book of Common Prayer"). What resulted was an armed insurrection, with many Scots signing the *[Solemn League and Covenant](/wiki/Solemn_League_and_Covenant "Solemn League and Covenant")*. The [Covenanters](/wiki/Covenanter "Covenanter") would serve as the government of Scotland for nearly a decade, and would also send military support to the [Parliamentarians](/wiki/Roundhead "Roundhead") during the [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War"). Following the restoration of the monarchy in 1660, [Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England"), despite the initial support that he received from the Covenanters, reinstated an episcopal form of government on the church.

However, with the [Glorious Revolution](/wiki/Glorious_Revolution "Glorious Revolution") of 1688 the Church of Scotland was unequivocally recognised as a Presbyterian institution by the monarch due to Scottish Presbyterian support for the aforementioned revolution and the [Acts of Union 1707](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1707 "Acts of Union 1707") between Scotland and England guaranteed the Church of Scotland's form of government. However, legislation by the [United Kingdom parliament](/wiki/United_Kingdom_parliament "United Kingdom parliament") allowing [patronage](/wiki/Patronage "Patronage") led to splits in the Church. In 1733, a group of ministers [seceded from the Church of Scotland](/wiki/First_Secession "First Secession") to form the Associate Presbytery, another group seceded in 1761 to form the [Relief Church](/wiki/Relief_Church "Relief Church") and the [Disruption of 1843](/wiki/Disruption_of_1843 "Disruption of 1843") led to the formation of the [Free Church of Scotland](/wiki/Free_Church_of_Scotland_(1843-1900) "Free Church of Scotland (1843-1900)"). Further splits took place, especially over theological issues, but most Presbyterians in Scotland were reunited by the 1929 union of the established Church of Scotland and the [United Free Church of Scotland](/wiki/United_Free_Church_of_Scotland "United Free Church of Scotland").

There are today ten Presbyterian denominations in Scotland. These are, listed by number of congregations within Scotland: the [Church of Scotland](/wiki/Church_of_Scotland "Church of Scotland"), the [Free Church of Scotland](/wiki/Free_Church_of_Scotland_(post_1900) "Free Church of Scotland (post 1900)"), the [United Free Church of Scotland](/wiki/United_Free_Church_of_Scotland "United Free Church of Scotland"), the [Free Presbyterian Church of Scotland](/wiki/Free_Presbyterian_Church_of_Scotland "Free Presbyterian Church of Scotland"), the [Free Church of Scotland (Continuing)](/wiki/Free_Church_of_Scotland_(Continuing) "Free Church of Scotland (Continuing)"), the [Didasko Presbytery](/wiki/Didasko "Didasko"),[[32]](#cite_note-32) the [Associated Presbyterian Church](/wiki/Associated_Presbyterian_Churches "Associated Presbyterian Churches"), the [Reformed Presbyterian Church of Scotland](/wiki/Reformed_Presbyterian_Church_of_Scotland "Reformed Presbyterian Church of Scotland"), the [International Presbyterian Church](/wiki/International_Presbyterian_Church "International Presbyterian Church") and two congregations of the [Free Presbyterian Church of Ulster](/wiki/Free_Presbyterian_Church_of_Ulster "Free Presbyterian Church of Ulster"). Combined, they have over 1500 congregations in Scotland.

Within Scotland the term "[kirk](/wiki/Kirk_(word) "Kirk (word)")" is usually used to refer to a local Presbyterian church. Informally, the term "The Kirk" refers to the Church of Scotland. Some of the values and ideals espoused in Scottish Presbyterian denominations can be reflected in this reference in a book from Norman Drummond, chaplain to the Queen in Scotland.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

#### England

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presbyterianism&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: England")]

Main article: [English Presbyterianism](/wiki/English_Presbyterianism "English Presbyterianism")

In England, Presbyterianism was established in secret in 1592. [Thomas Cartwright](/wiki/Thomas_Cartwright_(Puritan) "Thomas Cartwright (Puritan)") is thought to be the first Presbyterian in England. Cartwright's controversial lectures at [Cambridge University](/wiki/Cambridge_University "Cambridge University") condemning the [episcopal](/wiki/Episcopal_polity "Episcopal polity") hierarchy of the [Elizabethan](/wiki/Elizabethan_era "Elizabethan era") Church led to his deprivation of his post by Archbishop [John Whitgift](/wiki/John_Whitgift "John Whitgift") and his emigration abroad. Between 1645 and 1648, a series of ordinances of the [Long Parliament](/wiki/Long_Parliament "Long Parliament") established Presbyterianism as the polity of the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England"). Presbyterian government was established in London and Lancashire and in a few other places in England, although Presbyterian hostility to the [execution](/wiki/Execution_of_Charles_I "Execution of Charles I") of [Charles I](/wiki/Charles_I_of_England "Charles I of England") and the establishment of the republican [Commonwealth of England](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_England "Commonwealth of England") meant that Parliament never enforced the Presbyterian system in England. The [Restoration of the monarchy](/wiki/Stuart_Restoration "Stuart Restoration") in 1660 brought the return of [Episcopal church government](/wiki/Episcopal_polity "Episcopal polity") in England (and in Scotland for a short time); but the Presbyterian church in England continued in Non-Conformity, outside of the established church. In 1719 a major split, the [Salter's Hall controversy](/wiki/Thomas_Bradbury_(minister) "Thomas Bradbury (minister)"), occurred; with the majority siding with [nontrinitarian](/wiki/Nontrinitarian "Nontrinitarian") views. [Thomas Bradbury](/wiki/Thomas_Bradbury_(minister) "Thomas Bradbury (minister)") published several sermons bearing on the controversy, and in 1719, "An answer to the reproaches cast on the [dissenting ministers](/wiki/Dissenting_minister "Dissenting minister") who subscribed their belief of the Eternal Trinity." By the 18th century many English Presbyterian congregations had become [Unitarian](/wiki/Unitarianism "Unitarianism") in doctrine.

A number of new Presbyterian Churches were founded by [Scottish](/wiki/Scottish_people "Scottish people") immigrants to England in the 19th century and later. Following the 'Disruption' in 1843 many of those linked to the Church of Scotland eventually joined what became the [Presbyterian Church of England](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_of_England "Presbyterian Church of England") in 1876. Some, such as Crown Court (Covent Garden, London), St Andrew's (Stepney, London) and [Swallow Street](/wiki/Swallow_Street "Swallow Street") (London), did not join the English denomination, which is why there are Church of Scotland congregations in England such as those at [Crown Court](/wiki/Crown_Court_Church "Crown Court Church"), and [St Columba's](/wiki/St_Columba%27s_Church,_London "St Columba's Church, London"), Pont Street (Knightsbridge) in London. There is also a congregation in the heart of London's financial district called London City Presbyterian Church that is affiliated with the Free Church of Scotland.[[34]](#cite_note-34) The Free Presbyterian Church of Scotland also have a congregation in London,[[35]](#cite_note-35) as do the Free Presbyterian Church of Ulster – along with five others in England.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

In 1972, the [Presbyterian Church of England](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_of_England "Presbyterian Church of England") (PCofE) united with the [Congregational Church in England and Wales](/wiki/Congregational_Church_in_England_and_Wales "Congregational Church in England and Wales") to form the [United Reformed Church](/wiki/United_Reformed_Church "United Reformed Church") (URC). Among the congregations the PCofE brought to the URC were Tunley (Lancashire), [Aston Tirrold](/wiki/Aston_Tirrold "Aston Tirrold") (Oxfordshire) and John Knox Presbyterian Church, Stepney, London (now part of [Stepney Meeting House](/wiki/Stepney_Meeting_House "Stepney Meeting House") URC) – these are among the sole survivors today of the English Presbyterian churches of the 17th century. The URC also has a presence in Scotland, mostly of former [Congregationalist](/wiki/Congregational_church "Congregational church") Churches. Two former Presbyterian congregations, [St Columba's, Cambridge](/wiki/Downing_Place_United_Reformed_Church,_Cambridge#St_Columba's_Church "Downing Place United Reformed Church, Cambridge") (founded in 1879), and [St Columba's, Oxford](/wiki/St_Columba%27s_United_Reformed_Church,_Oxford "St Columba's United Reformed Church, Oxford") (founded as a chaplaincy by the PCofE and the [Church of Scotland](/wiki/Church_of_Scotland "Church of Scotland") in 1908 and as a congregation of the PCofE in 1929), continue as congregations of the URC and university chaplaincies of the [Church of Scotland](/wiki/Church_of_Scotland "Church of Scotland").

In recent years a number of smaller denominations adopting Presbyterian forms of church government have organised in England, including the [International Presbyterian Church](/wiki/International_Presbyterian_Church "International Presbyterian Church") planted by evangelical theologian [Francis Schaeffer](/wiki/Francis_Schaeffer "Francis Schaeffer") of the [L'Abri Fellowship](/wiki/L%27Abri "L'Abri") in the 1970s – now with fifteen English-speaking congregations in England, and 6 Korean-speaking congregations. There is also the [Evangelical Presbyterian Church in England and Wales](/wiki/Evangelical_Presbyterian_Church_in_England_and_Wales "Evangelical Presbyterian Church in England and Wales") founded in the North of England in the late 1980s.

#### Wales

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presbyterianism&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Wales")]

In [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales"), Presbyterianism is represented by the [Presbyterian Church of Wales](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_of_Wales "Presbyterian Church of Wales"), which was originally composed largely of [Calvinistic Methodists](/wiki/Calvinistic_Methodists "Calvinistic Methodists") who accepted Calvinist theology rather than the [Arminianism](/wiki/Arminianism "Arminianism") of the Wesleyan Methodists. They broke off from the Church of England in 1811, ordaining their own ministers. They were originally known as the Calvinist Methodist connexion and in the 1920s it became alternatively known as the Presbyterian Church of Wales.

#### Ireland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presbyterianism&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Ireland")]

Presbyterianism ([Irish](/wiki/Irish_language "Irish language"): *Preispitéireachas*, [Ulster Scots](/wiki/Ulster_Scots_dialect "Ulster Scots dialect"): *Prisbytairinism*) is the largest Protestant denomination in [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland") and the second largest on the island of Ireland (after the [Anglican](/wiki/Anglican "Anglican") [Church of Ireland](/wiki/Church_of_Ireland "Church of Ireland")),[[37]](#cite_note-37) and was brought by Scottish [plantation settlers](/wiki/Plantation_of_Ulster "Plantation of Ulster") to [Ulster](/wiki/Ulster "Ulster") who had been strongly encouraged to emigrate by James VI of Scotland, also [James I of Ireland and England](/wiki/James_I_of_England "James I of England"). After the start of the plantation of Ulster in 1606, several thousand Scottish Presbyterians moved to the northern counties of Ireland.[[38]](#cite_note-UHF-38) The first official [Presbyterian gathering](/wiki/Edward_Brice "Edward Brice") in Ireland was held in 1613.[[39]](#cite_note-39) The Presbytery of Ulster was formed in 1642 separately from the established Anglican Church; by 1659 there were nearly 80 presbyteries.[[38]](#cite_note-UHF-38) By 1715, the northern province of Ulster had a population of 600,000; more than 200,000 of these came from Scotland.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

Presbyterians, along with [Catholics](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic") in Ulster and the rest of Ireland, suffered under the discriminatory [Penal Laws](/wiki/Penal_Laws_against_Irish_Catholics "Penal Laws against Irish Catholics") until they were revoked in the early 19th century. Presbyterianism is represented in Ireland by the [Presbyterian Church in Ireland](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_in_Ireland "Presbyterian Church in Ireland"), the [Non-subscribing Presbyterian Church of Ireland](/wiki/Non-subscribing_Presbyterian_Church_of_Ireland "Non-subscribing Presbyterian Church of Ireland"), the [Free Presbyterian Church of Ulster](/wiki/Free_Presbyterian_Church_of_Ulster "Free Presbyterian Church of Ulster"), the [Reformed Presbyterian Church of Ireland](/wiki/Reformed_Presbyterian_Church_of_Ireland "Reformed Presbyterian Church of Ireland") and the [Evangelical Presbyterian Church](/wiki/Evangelical_Presbyterian_Church_(Ireland) "Evangelical Presbyterian Church (Ireland)").

#### France

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presbyterianism&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: France")]

There is a Church of Scotland (Presbyterian) in central Paris: [The Scots Kirk](/wiki/The_Scots_Kirk,_Paris "The Scots Kirk, Paris"), which is English-speaking, and is attended by many nationalities. It maintains close links with the Church of Scotland in Scotland itself, as well as with the [Reformed Church of France](/wiki/Reformed_Church_of_France "Reformed Church of France").

#### Italy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presbyterianism&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Italy")]

Further information: [Waldensians](/wiki/Waldensians "Waldensians")

The Waldensian Evangelical Church (Chiesa Evangelica Valdese, CEV) is an Italian Protestant denomination. The church was founded in the 12th century. After the Protestant Reformation, it adhered to Calvinist theology and became the Italian branch of the Presbyterian churches. As such, the church is a member of the World Communion of Reformed Churches.
