#### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presbyterianism&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: United States")]

See also: [Presbyterianism in the United States](/wiki/Presbyterianism_in_the_United_States "Presbyterianism in the United States")

Presbyterianism first officially arrived in Colonial America in 1644 with the establishment of Christ's First Presbyterian Church in Heemstede/ Hempstead, Nieuw Amsterdam/ New York. The Church was organized by the Rev. Richard Denton.

In 1703 the first Presbytery in Philadelphia was established. In time, the presbytery would be joined by two more to form a synod (1717) and would evolve into the [Presbyterian Church in the United States of America](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_in_the_United_States_of_America "Presbyterian Church in the United States of America") in 1789. The nation's largest Presbyterian denomination, the [Presbyterian Church (U.S.A.)](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_(U.S.A.) "Presbyterian Church (U.S.A.)") – PC (USA) – can trace its heritage to the original PCUSA, as can the [Presbyterian Church in America](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_in_America "Presbyterian Church in America") (PCA), the [Orthodox Presbyterian Church](/wiki/Orthodox_Presbyterian_Church "Orthodox Presbyterian Church") (OPC), the [Bible Presbyterian Church](/wiki/Bible_Presbyterian_Church "Bible Presbyterian Church") (BPC), the [Cumberland Presbyterian Church](/wiki/Cumberland_Presbyterian_Church "Cumberland Presbyterian Church") (CPC), the [Cumberland Presbyterian Church in America](/wiki/Cumberland_Presbyterian_Church_in_America "Cumberland Presbyterian Church in America"), the [Evangelical Presbyterian Church](/wiki/Evangelical_Presbyterian_Church_(United_States) "Evangelical Presbyterian Church (United States)") (EPC), and the [Evangelical Covenant Order of Presbyterians](/wiki/Evangelical_Covenant_Order_of_Presbyterians "Evangelical Covenant Order of Presbyterians") (ECO).

Other Presbyterian bodies in the United States include the [Reformed Presbyterian Church of North America](/wiki/Reformed_Presbyterian_Church_of_North_America "Reformed Presbyterian Church of North America") (RPCNA), the [Associate Reformed Presbyterian Church](/wiki/Associate_Reformed_Presbyterian_Church "Associate Reformed Presbyterian Church") (ARP), the [Reformed Presbyterian Church in the United States](/wiki/Reformed_Presbyterian_Church_in_the_United_States "Reformed Presbyterian Church in the United States") (RPCUS), the [Reformed Presbyterian Church General Assembly](/wiki/Reformed_Presbyterian_Church_General_Assembly "Reformed Presbyterian Church General Assembly"), the [Reformed Presbyterian Church – Hanover Presbytery](/wiki/Reformed_Presbyterian_Church_%E2%80%93_Hanover_Presbytery "Reformed Presbyterian Church – Hanover Presbytery"), the [Covenant Presbyterian Church](/wiki/Covenant_Presbyterian_Church "Covenant Presbyterian Church"), the Presbyterian Reformed Church, the Westminster Presbyterian Church in the United States, the [Korean American Presbyterian Church](/wiki/Korean_American_Presbyterian_Church "Korean American Presbyterian Church"), and the [Free Presbyterian Church of North America](/wiki/Free_Presbyterian_Church_of_North_America "Free Presbyterian Church of North America").

The territory within about a 50-mile (80 km) radius of [Charlotte, North Carolina](/wiki/Charlotte,_North_Carolina "Charlotte, North Carolina"), is historically the greatest concentration of Presbyterianism in the Southern United States, while an almost identical geographic area around [Pittsburgh](/wiki/Pittsburgh "Pittsburgh"), Pennsylvania, contains probably the largest number of Presbyterians in the nation.

The PC (USA), beginning with its predecessor bodies, has, in common with other so-called "mainline" Protestant denominations, experienced a significant decline in members in recent years. Some estimates have placed that loss at nearly half in the last forty years.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

Presbyterian influence, especially through [Princeton theology](/wiki/Princeton_theology "Princeton theology"), can be traced in modern [Evangelicalism](/wiki/Evangelicalism "Evangelicalism"). [Randall Balmer](/wiki/Randall_Balmer "Randall Balmer") says that:

> Evangelicalism itself, I believe, is a quintessentially North American phenomenon, deriving as it did from the confluence of [Pietism](/wiki/Pietism "Pietism"), Presbyterianism, and the vestiges of [Puritanism](/wiki/Puritanism "Puritanism"). Evangelicalism picked up the peculiar characteristics from each strain – warmhearted spirituality from the Pietists (for instance), doctrinal precisionism from the Presbyterians, and individualistic introspection from the Puritans – even as the North American context itself has profoundly shaped the various manifestations of evangelicalism: fundamentalism, neo-evangelicalism, the holiness movement, Pentecostalism, the charismatic movement, and various forms of African-American and Hispanic evangelicalism.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

— Randall Balmer, The Encyclopedia of Evangelicalism (2002)

In the late 1800s, Presbyterian missionaries established a presence in what is now northern [New Mexico](/wiki/New_Mexico "New Mexico"). This provided an alternative to the Catholicism, which was brought to the area by the Spanish [Conquistadors](/wiki/Conquistador "Conquistador") and had remained unchanged. The area experienced a "mini" reformation, in that many converts were made to Presbyterianism, prompting persecution. In some cases, the converts left towns and villages to establish their own neighboring villages. The arrival of the United States to the area prompted the Catholic church to modernize and make efforts at winning the converts back, many of which did return. However, there are still stalwart Presbyterians and Presbyterian churches in the area.

Lutherans, [Episcopalians](/wiki/Episcopal_Church_(USA) "Episcopal Church (USA)"), and Presbyterians historically tend to be considerably wealthier[[43]](#cite_note-THE_EPISCOPALIANS-43) and have been better educated (having more [graduate](/wiki/Academic_degree "Academic degree") and post-graduate degrees per capita) than most other religious groups in United States.[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45) They are disproportionately represented in the upper reaches of American business,[[46]](#cite_note-46) law, and politics.[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48)[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] They are part of a group known as [White Anglo-Saxon Protestants](/wiki/White_Anglo-Saxon_Protestants "White Anglo-Saxon Protestants") (WASPs).

#### Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presbyterianism&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Canada")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Rev_Bruin_Romcas_Comisco%2C_Lunenburg%2C_Nova_Scotia.png/250px-Rev_Bruin_Romcas_Comisco%2C_Lunenburg%2C_Nova_Scotia.png)](/wiki/File:Rev_Bruin_Romcas_Comisco,_Lunenburg,_Nova_Scotia.png)

Reverend [Bruin Romkes Comingo](/wiki/Bruin_Romkes_Comingo "Bruin Romkes Comingo"), 1st Presbyterian Minister in Canada, at [St. Andrew's Presbyterian Church](/wiki/St._Andrew%27s_Presbyterian_Church_(Lunenburg) "St. Andrew's Presbyterian Church (Lunenburg)") in [Lunenburg](/wiki/Lunenburg,_Nova_Scotia "Lunenburg, Nova Scotia")

In Canada, the largest Presbyterian denomination – and the largest Protestant denomination – was the [Presbyterian Church in Canada](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_in_Canada "Presbyterian Church in Canada"), formed in 1875 with the merger of four regional groups. In 1925, the [United Church of Canada](/wiki/United_Church_of_Canada "United Church of Canada") was formed by the majority of Presbyterians combining with the [Methodist Church](/wiki/Methodist_Church_(Canada) "Methodist Church (Canada)") and the [Congregational Union of Canada](/wiki/Congregational_church "Congregational church"). A sizable minority of Canadian Presbyterians, primarily in southern [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario") but also throughout the nation, withdrew, and reconstituted themselves as a non-concurring continuing Presbyterian body. They regained use of the original name in 1939.

### Latin America

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presbyterianism&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Latin America")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Catedral_Presbiteriana%2C_Rio_de_Janeiro.jpg/250px-Catedral_Presbiteriana%2C_Rio_de_Janeiro.jpg)](/wiki/File:Catedral_Presbiteriana,_Rio_de_Janeiro.jpg)

A Presbyterian cathedral in [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro"), Brazil

Presbyterianism arrived in Latin America in the 19th century.

#### Mexico

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presbyterianism&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Mexico")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/IglesiaSionSLP.jpg/250px-IglesiaSionSLP.jpg)](/wiki/File:IglesiaSionSLP.jpg)

[National Presbyterian Church](/wiki/National_Presbyterian_Church_in_Mexico "National Presbyterian Church in Mexico") in the historic center of [San Luis Potosí](/wiki/San_Luis_Potos%C3%AD_(city) "San Luis Potosí (city)"), Mexico

The biggest Presbyterian church is the [National Presbyterian Church in Mexico](/wiki/National_Presbyterian_Church_in_Mexico "National Presbyterian Church in Mexico") (*Iglesia Nacional Presbiteriana de México*), which has around 2,500,000 members and associates and 3000 congregations, but there are other small denominations like the Associate Reformed Presbyterian Church in Mexico which was founded in 1875 by the Associate Reformed Church in North America. The [Independent Presbyterian Church](/wiki/Independent_Presbyterian_Church_in_Mexico "Independent Presbyterian Church in Mexico"), the Presbyterian Reformed Church in Mexico, and the [National Conservative Presbyterian Church in Mexico](/wiki/National_Conservative_Presbyterian_Church_in_Mexico "National Conservative Presbyterian Church in Mexico") are existing churches in the Reformed tradition.

#### Brazil

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presbyterianism&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Brazil")]

In Brazil, the [Presbyterian Church of Brazil](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_of_Brazil "Presbyterian Church of Brazil") (*Igreja Presbiteriana do Brasil*) totals approximately 1,011,300 members;[[49]](#cite_note-49) other Presbyterian churches (Independents, United, Conservatives, Renovated, etc.) in this nation have around 350,000 members. The [Renewed Presbyterian Church in Brazil](/wiki/Renewed_Presbyterian_Church_in_Brazil "Renewed Presbyterian Church in Brazil") was influenced by the charismatic movement and has about 131 000 members as of 2011.[[50]](#cite_note-50) The [Conservative Presbyterian Church in Brazil](/wiki/Conservative_Presbyterian_Church_in_Brazil "Conservative Presbyterian Church in Brazil") was founded in 1940 and has eight presbyteries.[[51]](#cite_note-51) The Fundamentalist Presbyterian church in Brazil was influenced by [Carl McIntire](/wiki/Carl_McIntire "Carl McIntire") and the US [Bible Presbyterian Church](/wiki/Bible_Presbyterian_Church "Bible Presbyterian Church") and has around 1 800 members. The [Independent Presbyterian Church of Brazil](/wiki/Independent_Presbyterian_Church_of_Brazil "Independent Presbyterian Church of Brazil") was founded in 1903 by Rev. Eduardo Carlos Pereira, has 500 congregations and 75 000 members. The [United Presbyterian Church of Brazil](/wiki/United_Presbyterian_Church_of_Brazil "United Presbyterian Church of Brazil") has around 4 000 members. There are also ethnic Korean Presbyterian churches in the country. The [Evangelical Reformed Churches in Brazil](/wiki/Evangelical_Reformed_Churches_in_Brazil "Evangelical Reformed Churches in Brazil") has Dutch origin. The [Reformed Churches in Brazil](/wiki/Reformed_Churches_in_Brazil "Reformed Churches in Brazil") were recently founded by the [Canadian Reformed Churches](/wiki/Canadian_and_American_Reformed_Churches "Canadian and American Reformed Churches") with the [Reformed Churches in the Netherlands (Liberated)](/wiki/Reformed_Churches_in_the_Netherlands_(Liberated) "Reformed Churches in the Netherlands (Liberated)").

[Congregational](/wiki/Congregational_church "Congregational church") churches present in the country are also part of the Calvinistic tradition in Latin America.

#### Other Latin American states

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presbyterianism&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Other Latin American states")]

There are probably more than four million members of Presbyterian churches in all of Latin America. Presbyterian churches are also present in Peru, Bolivia, Cuba, Trinidad and Tobago, Venezuela, Colombia, Chile, Paraguay, Costa Rica, Nicaragua, Argentina, Honduras and others, but with few members. The Presbyterian Church in Belize has 17 churches and church plants and there is a Reformed Seminary founded in 2004. Some Latin Americans in North America are active in the [Presbyterian Cursillo Movement](/wiki/Presbyterian_Cursillo_Movement "Presbyterian Cursillo Movement").

### Africa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presbyterianism&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Africa")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/Christ_Presbyterian_Church_Akropong.jpg/250px-Christ_Presbyterian_Church_Akropong.jpg)](/wiki/File:Christ_Presbyterian_Church_Akropong.jpg)

[Christ Presbyterian Church](/wiki/Christ_Presbyterian_Church,_Akropong "Christ Presbyterian Church, Akropong") in [Akropong](/wiki/Akropong "Akropong"), Ghana

Presbyterianism arrived in Africa in the 19th century through the work of Scottish missionaries and founded churches such as [St Michael and All Angels Church, Blantyre, Malawi](/wiki/St_Michael_and_All_Angels_Church,_Blantyre,_Malawi "St Michael and All Angels Church, Blantyre, Malawi"). The church has grown extensively and now has a presence in at least 23 countries in the region.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

African Presbyterian churches often incorporate diaconal ministries, including social services, emergency relief, and the operation of mission hospitals. A number of partnerships exist between presbyteries in Africa and the PC(USA), including specific connections with Lesotho, Cameroon, Malawi, South Africa, Ghana and Zambia. For example, the Lackawanna Presbytery, located in Northeastern Pennsylvania, has a partnership with a presbytery in Ghana. Also the Southminster Presbyterian Church, located near Pittsburgh, has partnerships with churches in Malawi and Kenya. The Presbyterian Church of Nigeria, western Africa is also active in mostly the southern states of this nation with strong density in the south-eastern states. [Hope Waddel](/wiki/Hope_Masterton_Waddell "Hope Masterton Waddell")'s missionary expedition in the mid 19th century, and later [Mary Slessor](/wiki/Mary_Slessor "Mary Slessor")'s work in coastal regions of the then British colony has brought about the church in these areas.

**Cameroon**

The [Presbyterian Church in Cameroon](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_in_Cameroon "Presbyterian Church in Cameroon") is a member of reformed churches in Cameroon.

#### Kenya

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presbyterianism&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Kenya")]

The [Presbyterian Church of East Africa](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_of_East_Africa "Presbyterian Church of East Africa"), based in Kenya, has 500 clergy and 4 million members.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

#### Malawi

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presbyterianism&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Malawi")]

The Reformed Presbyterian Church in Malawi (RPCM) has 150 congregations and 17 000–20 000 members[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]. It was a mission of the Free Presbyterian church of Scotland. The Restored Reformed Church works with RPCM. Evangelical Presbyterian Church in Malawi is an existing small church. Part of the Presbyterian Church in Malawi and Zambia is known as CCAP, Church of Central Africa-Presbyterian. Often the churches there have one main congregation and a number of prayer houses. Education, health ministries, and worship and spiritual development are important.

#### Southern Africa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presbyterianism&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Southern Africa")]

The Bantu Presbyterian Church of South Africa was formed in 1923, following missionary work by the Free Church of Scotland with indigenous Africans at the Lovedale Mission.[[54]](#cite_note-54) In 1992, it was renamed as the Reformed Presbyterian Church.[[55]](#cite_note-55) In 1999, it united with the (historically European) Presbyterian Church in South Africa to form the [Uniting Presbyterian Church in Southern Africa](/wiki/Uniting_Presbyterian_Church_in_Southern_Africa "Uniting Presbyterian Church in Southern Africa").[[56]](#cite_note-56)[[57]](#cite_note-57)

#### Northern Africa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presbyterianism&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Northern Africa")]

* [The Presbyterian Church of South Sudan and Sudan](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_in_Sudan "Presbyterian Church in Sudan"), founded in 1902 by American missionaries in [Malakal](/wiki/Malakal "Malakal"), was deprived of foreign pastors since 1962 by decision of the Sudanese government but it continued to grow. It is now the 3rd largest Christian church in Sudan with 1,000,000 members, shared between [Sudan](/wiki/Sudan "Sudan") and [South Sudan](/wiki/South_Sudan "South Sudan").[[58]](#cite_note-PCSSS-58)
* The [Presbyterian Evangelical Church of Sudan](/wiki/Sudan_Evangelical_Presbyterian_Church "Sudan Evangelical Presbyterian Church") was founded in the north of the country and in Khartoum by the same American missionaries in the late 19th century but left under the guidance of Egyptian evangelical pastors of Coptic origin.[[58]](#cite_note-PCSSS-58)

In addition, there are a number of Presbyterian Churches in north Africa, the most known is the Nile Synod in Egypt and a recently founded synod for Sudan.

### Asia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presbyterianism&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Asia")]

#### Hong Kong

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presbyterianism&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Hong Kong")]

[The Hong Kong Council of the Church of Christ in China](/wiki/The_Hong_Kong_Council_of_the_Church_of_Christ_in_China "The Hong Kong Council of the Church of Christ in China") (CCC) is a uniting church formed by [Presbyterians](/wiki/Presbyterians "Presbyterians") and [Congregationalists](/wiki/Congregationalists "Congregationalists"), which inherited the [Reformed tradition](/wiki/Reformed_tradition "Reformed tradition"). [HKCCCC](/wiki/The_Hong_Kong_Council_of_the_Church_of_Christ_in_China "The Hong Kong Council of the Church of Christ in China") is also the only [mainline](/wiki/Mainline_Protestant "Mainline Protestant") Reformed church in Hong Kong.

[Cumberland Presbyterian Church Yao Dao Secondary School](/wiki/Cumberland_Presbyterian_Church_Yao_Dao_Secondary_School "Cumberland Presbyterian Church Yao Dao Secondary School") is a Presbyterian school in [Yuen Long](/wiki/Yuen_Long "Yuen Long"), [New Territories](/wiki/New_Territories "New Territories"). The [Cumberland Presbyterian Church](/wiki/Cumberland_Presbyterian_Church "Cumberland Presbyterian Church") also have a church on the island of [Cheung Chau](/wiki/Cheung_Chau "Cheung Chau"). There are also [Korean Christians](/wiki/Korean_Christians_in_Hong_Kong "Korean Christians in Hong Kong") resident in Hong Kong who are Presbyterians.[[59]](#cite_note-59)[[60]](#cite_note-60)

#### South Korea

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presbyterianism&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: South Korea")]

Main article: [Presbyterianism in South Korea](/wiki/Presbyterianism_in_South_Korea "Presbyterianism in South Korea")

Presbyterian Churches are the biggest and by far the most influential Protestant denominations in South Korea, with close to 20,000 churches affiliated with the two largest Presbyterian denominations in the country.[[61]](#cite_note-61) In South Korea there are 9 million Presbyterians, forming the majority of the 15 million Korean [Protestants](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant"). In South Korea there are 100 different Presbyterian denominations.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

Most of the Korean Presbyterian denominations share the same name in Korean, 대한예수교장로회 (literally means the Presbyterian Church of Korea or PCK), tracing its roots to the United Presbyterian Assembly. A Presbyterian schism began with a Japanese shrine worship enforced during the Japanese colonial period and the establishment of a minor division (Koryu-pa, 고려파, later The Koshin Presbyterian Church in Korea, Koshin 고신) in 1952. In 1953 a second schism happened when the theological orientation of the Chosun Seminary (later [Hanshin University](/wiki/Hanshin_University "Hanshin University")) founded in 1947 could not be tolerated in the PCK and another minor group (The [Presbyterian Church in the Republic of Korea](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_in_the_Republic_of_Korea "Presbyterian Church in the Republic of Korea"), Kijang, 기장) was separated. The last major schism had to do with the issue of whether the PCK should join the WCC. The controversy divided the PCK into two denominations, The Presbyterian Church of Korea (Tonghap, 통합) and The General Assembly of Presbyterian Church in Korea (Hapdong, 합동) in 1959. All major seminaries associated with each denomination claim heritage from the [Pyung Yang Theological Seminary](/wiki/Pyongyang_Theological_Seminary "Pyongyang Theological Seminary").[[63]](#cite_note-63)

Korean Presbyterian denominations are active in evangelism and many of its missionaries are being sent overseas, being the second biggest missionary sender in the world after the United States. GMS, the missionary body of the "Hapdong" General Assembly of Presbyterian Churches of Korea, is the single largest Presbyterian missionary organization in Korea.[[64]](#cite_note-64) In addition there are many Korean-American Presbyterians in the United States, either with their own church sites or sharing space in pre-existing churches as is the case in Australia, New Zealand and even Muslim countries such as Saudi Arabia with Korean immigration.

The Korean Presbyterian Church started through the mission of the Presbyterian Church (USA) and the Australian Presbyterian theological tradition is central to the United States. But after independence, the 'Presbyterian Church in Korea (KoRyuPa)' advocated a Dutch [Reformed](/wiki/Reformed_tradition "Reformed tradition") position. In the 21st century, a new General Assembly of the Orthodox Presbyterian Church of Korea (Founder. [Ha Seung-moo](/wiki/Ha_Seung-moo "Ha Seung-moo")) in 2012 declared itself an authentic historical succession of Scottish Presbyterian John Knox.

#### Taiwan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presbyterianism&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Taiwan")]

The [Presbyterian Church in Taiwan](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_in_Taiwan "Presbyterian Church in Taiwan") (PCT) is by far the largest Protestant denomination in [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan"), with some 238,372 members as of 2009 (including a majority of the island's [aborigines](/wiki/Formosan_Aboriginals "Formosan Aboriginals")). [English Presbyterian Missionary](/wiki/English_Presbyterian_Mission "English Presbyterian Mission") [James Laidlaw Maxwell](/wiki/James_Laidlaw_Maxwell "James Laidlaw Maxwell") established the first Presbyterian church in [Tainan](/wiki/Tainan "Tainan") in 1865. His colleague [George Leslie Mackay](/wiki/George_Leslie_Mackay "George Leslie Mackay"), of the [Canadian Presbyterian Mission](/wiki/Canadian_Presbyterian_Mission "Canadian Presbyterian Mission"), was active in [Tamsui](/wiki/Tamsui "Tamsui") and north Taiwan from 1872 to 1901; he founded the island's first university and hospital, and created a written script for [Taiwanese Minnan](/wiki/Taiwanese_Minnan "Taiwanese Minnan"). The English and Canadian missions joined as the PCT in 1912. One of the few churches permitted to operate in Taiwan through the era of Japanese rule (1895–1945), the PCT experienced rapid growth during the era of [Kuomintang](/wiki/Kuomintang "Kuomintang")-imposed martial law (1949–1987), in part due to its support for democracy, human rights, and [Taiwan independence](/wiki/Taiwan_independence "Taiwan independence"). Former [ROC](/wiki/Republic_of_China "Republic of China") president [Lee Teng-hui](/wiki/Lee_Teng-hui "Lee Teng-hui") (in office 1988–2000) was a Presbyterian.

#### India

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presbyterianism&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: India")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Jowai_Presbyterian_Church.JPG/250px-Jowai_Presbyterian_Church.JPG)](/wiki/File:Jowai_Presbyterian_Church.JPG)

Jowai Presbyterian Church in India

In the mainly Christian Indian state of [Mizoram](/wiki/Mizoram "Mizoram"), Presbyterianism is the largest of all [Christian denominations](/wiki/Christian_denomination "Christian denomination"). It was brought there by [missionaries](/wiki/Missionaries "Missionaries") from [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales") in 1897. Prior to Mizoram, Welsh Presbyterians started venturing into northeast India through the [Khasi Hills](/wiki/Khasi_Hills "Khasi Hills") (now in the state of [Meghalaya](/wiki/Meghalaya "Meghalaya") in India) and established Presbyterian churches all over the Khasi Hills from the 1840s onwards. Hence, there is a strong presence of Presbyterians in [Shillong](/wiki/Shillong "Shillong") (the present capital of Meghalaya) and the areas adjoining it. The Welsh missionaries built their first church in [Sohra](/wiki/Sohra "Sohra") (aka [Cherrapunji](/wiki/Cherrapunji "Cherrapunji")) in 1846. The Presbyterian church in India was integrated in 1970 into the [United Church of Northern India](/wiki/United_Church_of_Northern_India_%E2%80%93_Presbyterian_Synod "United Church of Northern India – Presbyterian Synod") (originally formed in 1924). It is the largest Presbyterian denomination in India.

### Oceania

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presbyterianism&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Oceania")]

#### Australia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presbyterianism&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Australia")]

See also: [List of Presbyterian denominations in Australia](/wiki/List_of_Presbyterian_denominations_in_Australia "List of Presbyterian denominations in Australia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/Timeline_of_Aust._Pres._Denominations.png/500px-Timeline_of_Aust._Pres._Denominations.png)](/wiki/File:Timeline_of_Aust._Pres._Denominations.png)

Timeline of the Presbyterian denominations in [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia") over the past century, and the movement of congregations from one to another

In [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), Presbyterianism is the fourth largest denomination of Christianity, with nearly 600,000 Australians claiming to be Presbyterian in the 2006 Commonwealth Census. Presbyterian churches were founded in each colony, some with links to the Church of Scotland and others to the Free Church. There were also congregations originating from United Presbyterian Church of Scotland as well as a number founded by [John Dunmore Lang](/wiki/John_Dunmore_Lang "John Dunmore Lang"). Most of these bodies merged between 1859 and 1870, and in 1901 formed a federal union called the [Presbyterian Church of Australia](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_of_Australia "Presbyterian Church of Australia") but retaining their state assemblies. The [Presbyterian Church of Eastern Australia](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_of_Eastern_Australia "Presbyterian Church of Eastern Australia") representing the Free Church of Scotland tradition, and congregations in Victoria of the [Reformed Presbyterian Church](/wiki/Reformed_Presbyterian_Church_of_Australia "Reformed Presbyterian Church of Australia"), originally from Ireland, are the other existing denominations dating from colonial times.

In 1977, about 70% of the Presbyterian Church of Australia, along with most of the [Congregational Union of Australia](/wiki/Congregational_Union_of_Australia "Congregational Union of Australia") and all the [Methodist Church of Australasia](/wiki/Methodist_Church_of_Australasia "Methodist Church of Australasia"), combined to form the [Uniting Church in Australia](/wiki/Uniting_Church_in_Australia "Uniting Church in Australia").[[65]](#cite_note-65) The 30% that did not unite had various reasons for so acting, often cultural attachment but often conservative theological or social views. The permission for the ordination of women given in 1974 was rescinded in 1991.[[66]](#cite_note-Rev-66) This did not affect the two or three existing woman ministers.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The approval of women elders given in the 1960s has been rescinded in most states.[[66]](#cite_note-Rev-66) The exception was New South Wales, which has the largest membership.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The theology of the church is now generally conservative and Reformed.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] A number of small Presbyterian denominations have arisen since the 1950s through migration or schism.

#### New Zealand

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presbyterianism&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: New Zealand")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/Kaikorai_Presbyterian_Church_Dunedin_New_Zealand.jpg/250px-Kaikorai_Presbyterian_Church_Dunedin_New_Zealand.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kaikorai_Presbyterian_Church_Dunedin_New_Zealand.jpg)

[Kaikorai Presbyterian Church](/wiki/Kaikorai_Presbyterian_Church "Kaikorai Presbyterian Church") in [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand")

In [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand"), Presbyterian is the dominant denomination in [Otago](/wiki/Otago "Otago") and [Southland](/wiki/Southland_Region "Southland Region") due largely to the rich [Scottish](/wiki/Scottish_people "Scottish people") and to a lesser extent [Ulster-Scots](/wiki/Ulster_Scots_people "Ulster Scots people") heritage in the region. The area around Christchurch, Canterbury, is dominated philosophically by the [Anglican](/wiki/Anglican "Anglican") denomination.

Originally there were two branches of Presbyterianism in New Zealand, the northern Presbyterian church which existed in the North Island and the parts of the South Island north of the [Waitaki River](/wiki/Waitaki_River "Waitaki River"), and the [Synod of Otago and Southland](/wiki/Synod_of_Otago_and_Southland "Synod of Otago and Southland"), founded by [Free Church](/wiki/Free_Church_of_Scotland_(1843%E2%80%931900) "Free Church of Scotland (1843–1900)") settlers in southern South Island. The two churches merged in 1901, forming what is now the [Presbyterian Church of Aotearoa New Zealand](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_of_Aotearoa_New_Zealand "Presbyterian Church of Aotearoa New Zealand").

In addition to the Presbyterian Church of Aotearoa New Zealand, there is also a more conservative Presbyterian church called [Grace Presbyterian Church of New Zealand](/wiki/Grace_Presbyterian_Church_of_New_Zealand "Grace Presbyterian Church of New Zealand"). Many of its members left the largely liberal PCANZ because they were seeking a more conservative church. It has 17 churches throughout New Zealand.

#### Vanuatu

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presbyterianism&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Vanuatu")]

In [Vanuatu](/wiki/Vanuatu "Vanuatu"), the [Presbyterian Church in Vanuatu](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_in_Vanuatu "Presbyterian Church in Vanuatu") is the largest denomination in the country, with approximately one-third of the population of Vanuatu members of the church. The PCV was taken to Vanuatu by missionaries from Scotland. The PCV (Presbyterian Church in Vanuatu) is headed by a moderator with offices in [Port Vila](/wiki/Port_Vila "Port Vila"). The PCV is particularly strong in the provinces of [Tafea](/wiki/Tafea "Tafea"), [Shefa](/wiki/Shefa_Province "Shefa Province"), and [Malampa](/wiki/Malampa "Malampa"). The Province of [Sanma](/wiki/Sanma_Province "Sanma Province") is mainly Presbyterian with a strong Catholic minority in the [Francophone](/wiki/Francophone "Francophone") areas of the province. There are some Presbyterian people, but no organised Presbyterian churches in [Penama](/wiki/Penama "Penama") and [Torba](/wiki/Torba_Province "Torba Province"), both of which are traditionally Anglican. Vanuatu is the only country in the South Pacific with a significant Presbyterian heritage and membership. The PCV is a founding member of yjr Vanuatu Christian Council (VCC). The PCV runs many primary schools and Onesua secondary school. The church is strong in the rural villages.
